Absolutely, it's possible to differentiate between running a Flask app as a server and as a CLI command, and to pass parameters to `create_app` without relying on environment variables. This can be achieved by leveraging the `app` object and its lifecycle, as well as by using the `flask` command-line interface's ability to inject the app into the command.

The key idea is that when you run `flask run`, the Flask app is initialized using `create_app`, and when you run a CLI command like `flask my_command`, the app is not started as a server, but rather as a command-line interface (CLI) application.

In your case, you can use the `flask` command-line interface to run your CLI commands, and in the same command, you can pass parameters to `create_app` without needing to set an environment variable. This would allow you to run your CLI command with a different `create_app` instance or with specific parameters, depending on the context in which the command is executed.

For example, you can define a CLI command that uses a different `create_app` instance or modifies its configuration based on the context. This can be done by using the `app` object that is passed into the CLI command, which is injected by the `flask` command-line interface.

You can also pass parameters to `create_app` when running the CLI command, such as specifying a different configuration or a different app instance. This would allow you to run the same command with different behavior depending on whether it's being run as a server or as a CLI command.

The `create_app` function can be called with different arguments or different configurations based on the context. For instance, you can pass a different configuration to `create_app` when running the CLI command, or you can pass a different app instance.

To achieve this without using environment variables, you can use the `flask` command-line interface's ability to inject the app into the command.
